charts.command: create a beak length distribution violin plot per species

modifications.selector: g[aria-label="Symbol legend titled 'Species' for fill color with 3 values: Adelie, Chinstrap, Gentoo"]

charts.command: remove the line between the panels

conversationHistory.content: Describe your modification.
remove the line between the panels
I've removed the divider lines between the violin panels by hiding the column header ticks/domain and the view stroke. Let me know if you'd also like to hide the panel background borders differently.

charts.command: remove the stack and show all distribution

conversationHistory.content: Describe your modification.
change this to area distribution chart

This is already a violin plot (a mirrored area density chart faceted by Species). Did you mean a standard overlapping area density chart — one shared plot with each species' distribution drawn as a filled curve? I've converted it to that: Beak Length on the x-axis, density on the y-axis, colored by species.
remove the stack and show all distribution

Done! I've converted it to a standard overlapping area density chart — each species' Beak Length distribution is drawn as a filled, semi-transparent curve on one shared plot, with no stacking so all distributions overlap for direct comparison.

modifications.selector: g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(4) > g:nth-of-type(2) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(2) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(1) > path[aria-label="density: 0.00546191491563; Beak Length (mm): 58; Species: Chinstrap"]

conversationHistory.content: Describe your modification.
add data points without jitter

I've added the raw beak length data points as a centered strip overlaid on each violin (no jitter). To do this I converted the chart to the 'facet' operator form so the violin density and the points can be layered per species without collapsing. You can tweak the point size, opacity, and color below.

modifications.selector: g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(4) > g:nth-of-type(2) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(2) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(1) > g:nth-of-type(1) > path[aria-label="density: 0.00546191491563; Beak Length (mm): 58; Species: Chinstrap"]

conversationHistory.content: Describe your modification.
add jitter
I added horizontal jitter to the overlaid raw data points so they spread out around the center of each violin instead of stacking in a single line. You can adjust the jitter width below.
Describe your modification.
add mean dashed line
I added a dashed vertical rule at the mean Beak Length for each species, colored to match. You can adjust the dash pattern and line width below.

csv-stocks.name: Stock Prices
csv-stocks.description: Monthly stock prices for 5 tech companies, 2000–2010 (symbol, date, price)

conversationHistory.content: Describe your modification.
widen the gap between the bars
I've widened the gap between bars by reducing the bar band width. You can adjust the padding — higher values mean wider gaps.
it didn't applied

Sorry about that! For grouped/stacked bars over a temporal axis, the gap is best controlled with a continuous bandwidth setting. I've set the bar's continuousBandSize to make each bar narrower, which widens the gaps. Lower values = thinner bars = wider gaps. Adjust it below.
it didn't applied

The issue is that your x-axis uses timeUnit 'month' as a continuous temporal scale, so band-based padding doesn't apply. To get real, controllable gaps between bars, I've made the month a discrete (ordinal) axis — now the bars sit in bands and 'barPadding' actually widens the gaps. Increase the value below for wider gaps (0 = no gap, 0.9 = very wide gaps).
Describe your modification.
sort as alphabet
I've sorted the weather categories alphabetically in the color legend and stacking order (drizzle, fog, rain, snow, sun).
Describe your modification.
add label middle of each data (white)
I added white count labels centered vertically within each stacked bar segment. You can tweak the label color and size below.
color and position didn't work

Thanks — the previous attempt didn't stack the labels to match the bars. I've fixed it: the text layer now uses the same stacked count so each white label sits centered inside its own weather segment. I converted the spec to a proper layer structure and set the text baseline to middle. You can adjust the label color and size below.
